title: VPN Not Working on Hotel or Airport WiFi? Here's How to Fix It
description: Captive portals, blocked UDP ports, and DPI kill most VPN connections on guest networks. Here's exactly what's happening and the specific fixes that work in 2026.
content:
## Why VPNs Break on Hotel and Airport WiFi

You connect to the hotel WiFi, launch your VPN, and it just spins. Or it connects for a second and drops. Or it connects but nothing loads. This isn't bad luck. It's how these networks are designed.

Hotel and airport networks have three specific characteristics that break standard VPN connections:

**Captive portals haven't been passed.** A captive portal is the login or terms page that appears before you get internet access. Until you accept the terms or enter your room number, the network only allows traffic to that specific portal page. Your VPN tries to connect and fails because the network isn't actually routing internet traffic yet. Your VPN client sees "connection failed" and retries. You see the VPN spinning forever.

**UDP ports are blocked.** Most hotel and airport networks allow TCP port 80 and TCP port 443 (standard web browsing) and sometimes UDP 443 (for QUIC/HTTP3). But ports like UDP 1194 (standard OpenVPN), UDP 51820 (WireGuard), UDP 500 and 4500 (IPSec), and others are often blocked entirely. If your VPN defaults to any of these, it will fail without telling you clearly why.

**Deep packet inspection on guest networks.** Higher-end hotels and most international airports run DPI on their guest networks. This isn't censorship, it's security and compliance. The firewall sees a WireGuard handshake or an OpenVPN certificate exchange and drops it as a policy decision.

## Fix 1: Pass the Captive Portal First

This is the most common fix and the one people skip.

1. Connect to the hotel or airport WiFi
2. **Turn your VPN off completely** if it's set to auto-connect
3. Open a browser and navigate to any HTTP site (not HTTPS). Try http://neverssl.com, which exists specifically for this purpose. Or try http://captive.apple.com. Your phone may do this automatically.
4. The captive portal should load. Accept the terms, enter your room number, or complete whatever authentication is required.
5. Confirm actual internet works: load a news site or check that a few pages open
6. Now reconnect your VPN

Many VPN apps have a "kill switch" feature that blocks all traffic when the VPN is off. If yours does, you may need to temporarily disable the kill switch to complete the captive portal step, then re-enable it after the VPN reconnects.

Some VPN apps auto-launch and try to connect before you pass the captive portal. If your VPN has an auto-connect feature, consider disabling it on untrusted networks and connecting manually after passing the portal.

## Fix 2: Switch to TCP Port 443

If the captive portal is passed and your VPN still doesn't connect, the problem is almost certainly a blocked port.

**TCP port 443 is the HTTPS port.** No network administrator can block TCP 443 without breaking normal web browsing, so it's universally allowed. Both OpenVPN and SSTP can run over TCP 443, making your VPN traffic look identical to a standard HTTPS web session.

How to switch in major VPN apps:

**NordVPN**: Go to Settings > Protocol and select OpenVPN TCP. NordVPN's automatic mode should fall back to this on its own if other protocols fail, but you can force it manually.

**Proton VPN**: Settings > Protocol, select OpenVPN (TCP). Or use Stealth, which already runs over TLS 443 by default.

**ExpressVPN**: The Lightway protocol normally runs over UDP. Go to Protocol settings and select OpenVPN (TCP) or Lightway (TCP).

**Surfshark**: Settings > VPN Protocol, select OpenVPN (TCP).

**Mullvad**: Settings > VPN Protocol, select OpenVPN with TCP on port 443. Or switch to Shadowsocks over TCP 443, which is even harder to block.

Note: TCP is slower than UDP for VPN use because TCP adds acknowledgment overhead on top of the VPN's existing encryption overhead. You're double-handling retransmissions. But "somewhat slower" beats "doesn't work."

## Fix 3: Try WireGuard on UDP 443

UDP 443 is increasingly allowed on guest networks because it carries QUIC/HTTP3 traffic from Google, YouTube, Cloudflare, and most major CDNs. Networks that block it break too much legitimate traffic.

Mullvad supports WireGuard on UDP port 443. In the Mullvad app, go to Settings > VPN Protocol, select WireGuard, and change the port to 443. This gives you WireGuard's speed with a port that passes most guest network firewalls.

NordVPN's NordLynx (WireGuard-based) also lets you specify ports in advanced settings on some platforms.

## Fix 4: Use an Obfuscated Protocol

If port switching alone doesn't work, the network is running DPI. It's not just blocking ports, it's inspecting what's inside your connections.

**NordVPN's obfuscated servers** use OpenVPN over SSL with traffic shaping that makes VPN handshakes look like browser traffic. Enable obfuscated servers in Settings > Advanced.

**Proton VPN's Stealth protocol** runs WireGuard inside TLS 1.3 and mimics browser fingerprints using uTLS. Hard to distinguish from a normal HTTPS session even under active inspection.

**Windscribe's Stealth and WStunnel protocols**: Stealth runs OpenVPN inside an SSL tunnel. WStunnel wraps VPN traffic inside a WebSocket over HTTPS. WebSocket is used by countless legitimate services (live chat, trading platforms, collaborative tools), so blocking it causes collateral damage networks prefer to avoid.

## Fix 5: Check Date and Time Settings

This sounds absurd but it comes up often enough to include. VPN authentication involves certificate validation, and certificate validation fails if your device clock is significantly wrong. When a device connects to a new network and picks up a different timezone or NTP server, the system clock can drift.

If your VPN connects but immediately drops, or gives a certificate error, check that your device's time is correct. On iOS, Settings > General > Date & Time, ensure "Set Automatically" is on. On Android, Settings > System > Date & time, enable automatic.

## Fix 6: Try a Different Server

Guest networks sometimes block specific VPN server IP ranges (common in countries with active censorship). Your VPN client may have successfully connected on the protocol level but chosen a server whose IP is on a blocklist.

Manually select a different server, ideally in a different country or region than what the app auto-selected. If you're in Europe, try connecting to a North American server instead of another European one. Different IP ranges have different block rates on guest networks.

## Fix 7: Use Your Phone's Mobile Data as a Hotspot

This isn't a VPN fix, it's a bypass. If hotel WiFi is too locked down and you have mobile data, tether your laptop to your phone's hotspot. Mobile carrier networks are almost never as restrictive as hotel guest networks, and your VPN will connect normally.

This is also the right call for airport WiFi if you're in a country where the airport applies national-level censorship to its WiFi (China's airports, for example, apply the same GFW filters as any other internet connection).

## Quick Diagnostic Checklist

Work through these in order:

1. Is the captive portal passed? Load http://neverssl.com to check.
2. Is TCP port 443 selected in your VPN protocol settings?
3. Is the kill switch blocking you from passing the captive portal? Temporarily disable it.
4. Try a manually selected server in a different country
5. Enable obfuscation mode if your VPN has it
6. Check your device's date and time
7. Try a completely different protocol (switch from WireGuard to OpenVPN TCP or vice versa)
8. If none of the above work, switch to mobile data

## Which VPNs Handle Hotel WiFi Best

**NordVPN** handles this situation better than most competitors because its automatic protocol mode tries multiple fallbacks: NordLynx first, then OpenVPN UDP, then OpenVPN TCP, then obfuscated servers. It usually lands on something that works without manual intervention.

**Proton VPN** with Stealth protocol is the strongest option for networks running active DPI. The TLS 1.3 tunneling with browser fingerprint mimicry is genuinely hard for guest network firewalls to classify as VPN traffic.

For general travel use, NordVPN's auto mode is convenient. For networks in restrictive countries or with aggressive DPI, Proton VPN's Stealth protocol is the more reliable technical solution.
